title: Decoder trained-weight tiny quality gate
hypothesis: The bf16 reciprocal PWL path plus logit score tie-break recovered exact-next behavior on the synthetic tiny decoder harness, but trained model weights may change logit margins and rank sensitivity. A small trained GPT-2-family ONNX model should be the next gate before scaling the same approximation path to distilgpt2, GPT-2, or larger array studies.

direct_comparison.primary_question: Does the current decoder bf16/PWL plus logit tie-break recovery remain exact-safe on a trained tiny GPT-2-family decoder workload?
include: reference ONNX generation from a trained tiny GPT-2-family checkpoint
exact reference and approximated candidate next-token traces
q12 exact-normalization and q8 reciprocal controls
bf16/PWL baseline and bf16/PWL logit tie-break recovery rows
exclude: training, fine tuning, or quantization-aware training

expected_benefit: separates trained-weight distribution risk from the previous random-model and prompt-proxy screens
provides a cheap pass/fail gate before importing larger decoder checkpoints
keeps the evaluator workflow on the existing decoder quality harness
risks: the model is intentionally tiny and may still have weak language quality
a pass here does not prove behavior on larger hidden sizes, deeper layer stacks, or real production distributions
the test remains next-token quality evidence rather than a full task-accuracy benchmark

required_evaluations.objective: Check whether bf16/PWL plus logit tie-break recovery remains exact-safe on a trained tiny GPT-2-family decoder workload.
